lesson_title: User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator

cards.text: To define your own namespace, use the {{c1::namespace}} keyword followed by the namespace name and a block.
cards.source: 7.2 - User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator

cards.back: Naming collisions - when two identical identifiers are introduced into the same scope and the compiler cannot disambiguate which one to use.
cards.source: 7.2 - User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator

cards.text: To access a namespace member, use the {{c1::scope resolution operator}} ({{c2:::}}) between the namespace name and identifier.
cards.source: 7.2 - User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator

cards.question: How do you call the add() function from the foo namespace?
cards.explanation: Use the scope resolution operator (::) to access members of a namespace: namespace_name::identifier
cards.source: 7.2 - User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator

cards.back: Scope resolution operator - used to access members of a namespace or class
cards.source: 7.2 - User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator

cards.front: Can you use the scope resolution operator (::) with no namespace name on the left?
cards.back: Yes. The scope resolution operator with no namespace name accesses the global namespace explicitly (e.g., ::doSomething()).
cards.source: 7.2 - User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator

cards.front: Can namespaces be nested inside other namespaces?
cards.back: Yes. Namespaces can be nested inside other namespaces. Access nested members using multiple scope resolution operators (e.g., foo::goo::add()).
cards.source: 7.2 - User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator

cards.text: Nested namespace foo::goo can be written more concisely in C++17 as {{c1::namespace foo::goo}}.
cards.source: 7.2 - User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator

cards.front: Do forward declarations need to be inside the same namespace as the function definition?
cards.back: Yes. Forward declarations must be inside the same namespace, or you must prefix the name with the namespace when declaring/defining (e.g., void foo::doSomething()).
cards.source: 7.2 - User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator

cards.front: Can a namespace be defined across multiple files?
cards.back: Yes. Namespaces can be defined across multiple files. All declarations within the same namespace are considered part of that namespace, regardless of which file they're in.
cards.source: 7.2 - User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: The definition is not in the foo namespace. It should be 'int foo::doSomething(int x, int y)' or be inside the namespace block.
cards.explanation: When defining a function declared in a namespace outside that namespace, you must prefix the function name with the namespace and scope resolution operator.
cards.source: 7.2 - User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator

cards.concept: Why use namespaces in your own programs
cards.explanation: Namespaces prevent naming collisions by grouping related identifiers under a unique name. As programs grow, the number of identifiers increases linearly, but potential collisions increase exponentially. Namespaces keep identifiers organized and separate.
examples: namespace math { int add(int x, int y); }
namespace graphics { int add(Point a, Point b); }
cards.source: 7.2 - User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator

cards.text: To define a namespace: {{c1::namespace}} identifier {{c2::{}} declarations {{c3::}}}
cards.source: 7.2 - User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator

cards.text: In C++17, nested namespaces can be written concisely: {{c1::namespace parent::child { }}} instead of nesting blocks.
cards.source: 7.2 - User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator

cards.text: A {{c1::naming collision}} occurs when two identical identifiers are introduced into the same {{c2::scope}}, preventing the compiler from disambiguating.
cards.source: 7.2 - User-defined namespaces and the scope resolution operator
